abstract: This survey examines how Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 can be leveraged to automate data annotation and synthesis, tasks that have traditionally been labor-intensive and costly. It positions itself as the first dedicated review of LLM-based annotation and synthesis, distinguishing itself from prior surveys focused on LLM architecture or general training. The paper organizes the field around methodologies for LLM-generated annotations, learning strategies that use such annotations, and challenges or ethical issues that arise.
key_claims: LLMs offer an unprecedented opportunity to automate data annotation and synthesis processes that were previously manual and expensive.
A dedicated synthesis of LLM-based annotation methods is currently missing from the literature, despite the rapid growth of work in this area.
Effective use of LLM-generated annotations requires careful methodological choices in both how data is generated and how downstream models learn from it.
There are distinctive challenges and ethical concerns associated with using LLMs as annotators that warrant focused attention.
methods: Literature survey and taxonomy-building covering LLM-based data annotation and synthesis.
Categorization along three primary axes: (1) LLM-Based Annotation Generation, (2) LLM-Generated Annotations Assessment, and (3) LLM-Generated Annotations Utilization.
Review of learning paradigms that consume LLM-generated annotations, with a typology including supervised settings and other downstream uses.
findings: The literature can be coherently organized into generation, assessment, and utilization of LLM-produced annotations.
Multiple learning strategies have emerged for leveraging LLM-generated labels in training downstream models.
Recurring challenges include quality assurance of generated annotations and ethical risks tied to automated labeling.
contributions: Provides what the authors describe as the first comprehensive survey focused specifically on LLM-based data annotation and synthesis.
Proposes a structured taxonomy of methodologies spanning annotation generation, assessment, and downstream utilization.
Reviews learning strategies that integrate LLM-generated annotations into model training.
Identifies open challenges and ethical considerations to guide future research in the area.
framing: The paper situates itself at the intersection of LLM research and the long-standing problem of data labeling in machine learning, positioning LLM-based annotation as a distinct subfield deserving its own synthesis. It is framed as complementary to existing surveys on LLM architecture and training, filling a gap by focusing on annotation and synthesis as application domains.
